critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: The 4.5% estimated program value improvement demonstrates challenger superiority over the incumbent.
Why wrong: The incumbent and challenger are evaluated under different business objectives. The incumbent is calibrated for renewal probability; the challenger is measured on net contribution margin. A policy optimized for margin will outperform a renewal-probability policy when judged on margin — that is expected. This comparison does not establish that the challenger is a better policy in general.
Evidence: Re-calibrate the incumbent on the margin objective and compare both policies on one frozen profit metric. If the gap shrinks materially, objective misalignment was the primary driver.

Issue 2
Claim: The doubly robust evaluator provides an unbiased causal estimate.
Why wrong: Nuisance models for outcome and propensity were fit on all four quarters including Q3-Q4. The reporting period is Q3-Q4. The evaluator has seen the data it is evaluating — this is in-sample evaluation, not holdout evaluation. The 4.5% estimate is not independent.
Evidence: Re-fit nuisance models on Q1-Q2 only and evaluate on Q3-Q4 as a true holdout. Compare estimates.

Issue 3
Claim: The operating point reflects a generalizable business threshold.
Why wrong: The cutoff was selected to maximize margin on Q3-Q4, then reported on Q3-Q4. This is circular — optimal performance on the threshold-selection set is guaranteed to exceed out-of-sample performance.
Evidence: Select threshold on Q1-Q2 validation data and report on Q3-Q4.

Issue 4
Claim: The comparison reflects the current quality of both policies.
Why wrong: The challenger had three rounds of Bayesian search. The incumbent has had no reoptimization in 18 months. Comparing a freshly tuned challenger to a stale incumbent is not a fair assessment of which policy design is better.
defender_raw:
Issue 1 (objective): The Finance-approved margin metric is the correct business metric for renewal-program ROI. The renewal probability scorecard was adopted before costs were analyzed at current scale. The shift to margin is not arbitrary — it reflects operational learning. The relevant comparison is whether the new targeting policy generates more profit per subscriber, not whether it scores higher on a metric the business has moved away from.

Issue 2 (DR estimator contamination): The causal identification in doubly robust estimation comes from the randomized experiment assignment, not from the nuisance model fit. If treatment was randomized within region-week cells (as stated), the causal estimate is valid conditional on correct propensity specification. Using more data (all four quarters) to fit the propensity model may actually improve, not degrade, the causal estimate.

Issue 3 (threshold self-selection): Concede. The cutoff should have been preselected based on earlier-quarter data before the Q3-Q4 window was finalized as the evaluation period. This is a legitimate benchmark hygiene failure.

Issue 4 (asymmetric optimization): Concede. A symmetric comparison requires re-running the incumbent through the same Bayesian search on the margin metric. The 4.5% may partly reflect optimization effort rather than policy quality.
adjudication_raw: The defender concedes Issues 3 and 4 and makes a partial rebuttal on Issue 2 (DR causal identification from randomization is valid; nuisance model training with all quarters reduces variance). The critic's Issue 2 concern is specifically about in-sample numerical inflation, not causal identification — the defender's rebuttal addresses a different concern. Issue 1 is contested: defender argues margin is the correct metric, not a defect. This is a fair point for the business choice but does not resolve the comparison asymmetry. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: objective changed from renewal probability to net contribution margin
Issue 2: DR nuisance models fit on full experiment log including Q3-Q4 reporting period
Issue 3: operating cutoff selected on Q3-Q4 cohort used for headline reporting
Issue 4: asymmetric optimization — Bayesian tuning for challenger, frozen incumbent

empirical_test.condition: Refit nuisance models on pre-reporting data, preselect threshold, re-calibrate incumbent to margin objective, evaluate both policies on frozen holdout with block-aware uncertainty
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Challenger lift falls below zero or CI includes zero on clean holdout against re-optimized incumbent
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Challenger beats re-optimized incumbent by at least 3% incremental profit with block-aware 95% CI excluding zero
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Positive but sub-3% lift, or heterogeneous regional gains including some negatives
